title: 1981-04-16_Pat_139.txt
key_claims: Applying Nicolas Malebranche's philosophy, Dick concludes that in 3-74 objects were transformed into intelligible Ideas. Because finite things cannot be experienced directly, God presents us with surrogate representations (icons) of the unitary Archetypal Ideas held in His mind.

The core of the 2-74 meta-abstraction is recognizing: "Not two mothers, but one mother twice." There is only one eternal Idea of an object; it is reused infinitely in different times and places. Plurality is an illusion.

Schizophrenia is the misapplication of this meta-abstraction: confounding two genuinely different objects/people as being one single entity. For example, Dick realizing that "Sandra" is actually two distinct personalities confounded into one concept.
*A Scanner Darkly* was an early, unconscious exploration of this exact cognitive dysfunction—the failure to properly distinguish between identities.
recurring_concepts: **The One Rose**: Using medieval philosophy, Dick illustrates that there is only one actual "Rose" (the archetype in God's mind). What we experience are pluralized icons/reflections of that single Idea.
reading_excerpt: Applying Nicolas Malebranche's philosophy, Dick concludes that in 3-74 objects were transformed into intelligible Ideas. Because finite things cannot be experienced directly, God presents us with surrogate representations (icons) of the unitary Archetypal Ideas held in His mind.
